text: The surgeon begins by dividing the gastrocolic ligament along the distal transverse colon, extending the dissection up to the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon exposes the second portion of the duodenum, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon initiates right colonic mobilization by incising the white lines of Toldt, which are visible as pale linear structures along the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon divides the attachments of the hepatic flexure, separating it from surrounding structures.

Following the complete mobilization of the hepatic flexure, the surgeon exposes the second portion of the duodenum and the pancreatic head, revealing these anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies upward traction on the appendix to dissect the right colon away from the right iliac fossa, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
Following complete mobilization of the right colon, the surgeon creates a window lateral to the second portion of the duodenum, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon pulls the transverse colon through the previously created window lateral to the second portion of the duodenum.
The surgeon performs an intracorporeal side-to-side anastomosis, connecting two sections of the bowel within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic total mesorectal excision (TME) and Deloyer procedure, manipulating the rectal tissue and surrounding structures within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies left colon ischemia following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, with visible changes in tissue coloration indicating compromised blood flow.
The surgeon prepares to perform a deloyer procedure due to insufficient transverse colon length for a tension-free anastomosis.
The surgeon exposes the geotaphasia and duodenums during the retro-ideal trans-mesenteric anastomosis, revealing these anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to divide the metabolic vessel trunk, which is necessary for the procedure in this case.
Following the ligation of metabolic vessels, the surgeon exposes the duodenum and pancreatic head, revealing these anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The transverse colon appears ischemic following the ligation of the metabolic vessels, with visible changes in tissue color indicating compromised blood flow.
The surgeon performs a counterclockwise rotation of the right colon, positioning the cecum beneath the liver and directing the transverse colon toward the pelvis.
Following the rotation of the colon, the surgeon resects the ischemic portion and inserts the anvil extracorporeally, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end anastomosis using a CDH-XXI stapler, joining the resected bowel ends together.
